{
  "decision": "fail",
  "risk": "high",
  "work_stage": "rough-complete",
  "component_conditions": ["installed-defective", "failed"],
  "percent_complete": 70,
  "value": 0,
  "sound_source": "",
  "confidence": 0.9,
  "event_time": 0,
  "rate": 0,
  "order": [],
  "workable": [],
  "findings": [
    "Milkshake oil cap — tan/cream emulsified residue on oil filler cap indicating coolant mixing with engine oil",
    "Visible milky sludge on underside of cap and around filler neck",
    "Internal coolant leak suspected — failed head gasket, cracked cylinder head, or compromised oil cooler",
    "Emulsified oil condition will cause bearing damage and lubrication failure if engine is operated"
  ],
  "actions": [
    "Do not start or run the engine — lock out powertrain for further diagnosis",
    "Perform coolant system pressure test to locate internal leak source",
    "Inspect cylinder head gasket, cylinder head for cracks, and oil cooler integrity",
    "Drain and replace contaminated oil and filter after repair",
    "Route unit to rework station for teardown and corrective repair before quality gate release"
  ],
  "rationale": "A milkshake oil cap is a definitive indicator of coolant-oil emulsion inside the engine, meaning a sealing failure has allowed coolant into the lubrication system. At rough-complete stage this is an installed-defective condition that must fail the quality gate — running the engine risks catastrophic bearing failure. OEM specs require clean, uncontaminated oil and no cross-fluid leakage at assembly quality checkpoints.",
  "references": ["OEM specs — powertrain fluid contamination acceptance criteria", "OEM specs — engine assembly quality gate leak and fluid inspection requirements"]
}
